texto: For a long time, I used to think that I had a man's brain that I thought more like a man than a woman. But now I've come to realise that whatever it is I do think like, it's not like men; because men don't really think like men, they think like boys.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a David Baddiel: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de limites e escolhas conscientes. O trecho “For a long time, I used to think that I had a man's brain that I thought more like a man than a w…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, boys, men pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e men.

frase_original: For a long time, I used to think that I had a man's brain that I thought more like a man than a woman. But now I've come to realise that whatever it is I do think like, it's not like men; because men don't really think like men, they think like boys.

texto: Yeah! "I love you" is subject to the law of diminishing returns; like one or two other critical weekly elements of a relationship, it loses a bit of thrilling value every time you get it out.'... That's what happens with "I love you", that same phrase that you once shouted Hollywood or Heathcliff-like in the lashing raining, now- now you are saying it dumbly at the end of every phone conversation, a follow-on from," I'll be back for dinner." Once it came out spontaneous rush, it forced itself out; now it's reflex.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a David Baddiel: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de vínculos afetivos e cuidado mútuo. O trecho “Yeah! "I love you" is subject to the law of diminishing returns; like one or two other critical w…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, daily-life, love pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de dia-chuvoso e termino.

frase_original: Yeah! "I love you" is subject to the law of diminishing returns; like one or two other critical weekly elements of a relationship, it loses a bit of thrilling value every time you get it out.'... That's what happens with "I love you", that same phrase that you once shouted Hollywood or Heathcliff-like in the lashing raining, now- now you are saying it dumbly at the end of every phone conversation, a follow-on from," I'll be back for dinner." Once it came out spontaneous rush, it forced itself out; now it's reflex.

texto: That's the thing about your destiny how are you supposed to know when it arrives? How are you supposed to recognise it from random life?
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de David Baddiel que provoca sentido da existência e experiência vivida. Ao questionar “That's the thing about your destiny how are you supposed to know when it arrives? How are you sup…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, destiny, supposed, com foco em reflexao.
